title: Chapter Two: The First Ghost
content: The banging started at 7 AM.

Maeve peeled her face off the pillow — she'd fallen asleep in last night's eyeliner again, and the pillowcase now wore a smudged portrait of her misery — and stumbled toward the door in an oversized Black Sabbath shirt and underwear. No pants. No bra. No dignity left to protect at this hour.
Chloe stood in the hallway holding two coffees and a stack of papers that had been folded, unfolded, and refolded so many times they looked like archaeological artifacts.
"You're not dressed." Chloe pushed past her without waiting for an invitation. The cold October air came with her, sharp and leaf-rot sweet, and Maeve shivered as it hit her bare legs.
"It's seven. In the morning. On a Saturday."

"I know. I literally couldn't sleep." Chloe set the coffees on the kitchen counter — a strip of laminate three steps from the bed, because attic apartments don't believe in walls — and thrust the papers at Maeve's chest. "I printed his essay."
Maeve blinked. The coffee smell cut through the fog in her brain, dark and bitter. She took a cup. "Whose essay?"

"Julian's. The one from the lecture. On epistolary intimacy." Chloe was already curling onto the foot of Maeve's unmade bed, tucking her legs beneath her in that boneless way she had, like a cat made of sunshine and entitlement. "I read it. Well. I read some of it. I highlighted the important parts."

Maeve looked down at the papers. Yellow highlighter blazed across nearly every line — but only the lines with long words. *Epistolary.* *Ontological.* *Ineffability.* The actual arguments, the connective tissue between the vocabulary, lay untouched, pale and unmarked as bone.
"Chloe."
"I know." Chloe pulled a pillow across her lap and hugged it. "I know it's bad. I literally couldn't tell which parts were the point and which parts were just — word decorations."

"Word decorations." Maeve sat on the desk chair, the one with the wheel that squeaked, and started reading. Julian's prose was precise and cool, each sentence a sealed room you had to find the hidden door of. He wrote about letter-writing as an act of radical self-erasure — how the epistolary form demands you construct a self on the page that can never fully match the self who sits bleeding at the desk. He cited Nietzsche. He cited Rilke's *Letters to a Young Poet*. He made an argument about desire being shaped by absence, about how we want most what we cannot see whole.
It was brilliant. It was also the kind of thing Chloe would drown in.
Maeve set the papers down gently, as if they might bite. "What do you want me to do with this?"
Chloe's eyes went wide and bright — the look she got when she was about to say something she'd been rehearsing. "You write to him. As me. Just one message."
The coffee turned acidic in Maeve's throat. "No."
"Maeve—"

"No. That's insane. That's dishonest, it's manipulative, and it won't even work. He's a literature professor, Chloe. He'll know in two sentences that the person writing isn't the same person who highlighted *ineffability* as a key term."
"You could make it sound like me. You're a writer—"
"Making it sound like you would mean making it sound like someone who can't parse his essay. What's the point of that?"

Chloe's face shifted. The brightness cracked, just at the edges, and underneath was something raw and desperate that Maeve had never seen before. Her best friend, who had sailed through twenty-one years on the current of her own face, looked stranded.

"What if he never sees the real me either way?" Chloe said. Her voice was small. Not performance-small. Real small. "What if the only version of me he can perceive is — the blonde one? The decorative one? At least this way, someone's words reach him. Even if they're not technically mine."
That landed like a fist.

Maeve turned the coffee cup in her hands, feeling the heat bleed into her palms. The radiator clanked once, twice — her attic was always cold, always noisy, always one inconvenience away from uninhabitable. She thought about Chloe at the salon, leaning forward in her chair like a sunflower toward light, and Julian's eyes sliding right past her as if she were glass.

She also thought about herself. Standing two feet away. Also invisible, but differently. Chloe was invisible because she was too legible — everyone read her surface and assumed the book was shallow. Maeve was invisible because no one opened the book at all.
"Even if I wanted to," Maeve said slowly, "how would it work? You can't exactly start a conversation with a professor using his academic essay as a dating profile."

Chloe reached for her phone. "I made an account. Anonymous-ish. Look." She held up the screen: @cHL0E_reads. One post. A photo of a book — Rilke, spine cracked, lying on a wool blanket — with no face visible. No identifying details. Just the book, the blanket, the suggestion of a person who read with her hands.
"That's actually—" Maeve caught herself. "That's not terrible."
"I know. I literally spent four hours on the aesthetic. The blanket is from my grandma's house." Chloe swiped. "See, I can *do* the visual part. It's the words part I can't—"
"You can. You just—"

"Maeve. I literally highlighted *ontological* because it had the most letters." Chloe's laugh was thin and bruised. "I sat with that essay for three hours. Three. I read every sentence twice. I still can't tell you what it means except something about letters and wanting people you can't see. Which, fine, I got that part. But I can't *say* it the way he'd want to hear it."
The silence between them was full of things Maeve didn't want to admit.

She knew what would happen if she said no. Chloe would try anyway. She'd cobble together some earnest, misspelled message full of exclamation points and the word "literally," and Julian — precise, cutting, sapiosexual Julian — would read it the way you read a misspelled billboard: with a wince and a dismissal. And Chloe would come back to this attic with that same cracked-bright look, only worse, because hope that's been acknowledged and then destroyed hurts more than hope that never left the ground.
The image unspooled: Chloe, beautiful and drowning, trying to discuss Rilke with a man who thought in footnotes. It was painful. Not funny-painful. Ache-painful.

And Maeve knew — with the cold clarity she usually reserved for dismantling her own reflection — that she could write something that would make him listen. She could build a voice out of Chloe's fragments, make it warm and searching and just unschooled enough to be real. She could weave in the references that would catch his attention like a hook through the lip. She could make him *see* the person behind the account.
But writing for Chloe meant erasing herself again. Putting words in someone else's mouth. Being the ghost behind the face.
She'd spent her whole life being the ghost.
"One message," Maeve said.
Chloe's whole body went still. "Really?"
"One. And then we see what happens. If he doesn't reply, we delete the account and never speak of this again."

"He'll reply." Chloe was already off the bed, already hugging her, already pressing her cheek against Maeve's shoulder in that careless affectionate way that made Maeve simultaneously grateful and aware of every inch of space her body occupied. "You're literally the best writer I know. He's going to fall in love with — with the words. And then I'll meet him, and I'll be the person behind the words, and—"
"Chloe." Maeve held up a hand. "One message."
Chloe left at noon. She'd given Maeve the login for @cHL0E_reads on a sticky note shaped like a strawberry, as if this were a fun craft project and not a fraud.
Maeve didn't start writing.

She cleaned the apartment. She reorganized her books by color, then by spine height, then — ashamed of herself — by the Library of Congress system. She ate cold rice from a takeout container and stared at the blinking cursor on her laptop and did not write.
At 8 PM, she opened Julian's essay again.

At 10 PM, she reread it with a pen in her hand, and this time she annotated it properly — the argument's architecture, the nested claims, the moment in paragraph four where he tilted from analysis into something almost personal. *The letter is the body's proxy. To write to someone is to give them a version of yourself that can be held, reread, possessed — a self that cannot look away or leave the room.*
She circled that sentence twice.
At 11 PM, she opened a blank document and wrote: *Dear Professor Adler—*
No. Too formal. Too student. He'd smell the assignment from the first comma.
She deleted it.

At midnight, she poured herself two fingers of the cheap bourbon she kept in a drawer and sat with her feet on the desk and the laptop on her thighs, and she let herself think about what she would say if she could say anything. Not as Chloe. As herself. If she could sit across from Julian Adler in some candle-thick bar and tell him what his essay actually meant — not the academic scaffold but the bleeding thing underneath.

He was wrong about one thing. He wrote that the letter is the body's proxy. But the letter isn't a proxy. The letter is a *confession* the body can't make. The body lies through its posture and its beauty and its terrible, democratic surface. The letter — the real letter, the one that costs something — is the only place you can tell the truth, because it's the only place you're already admitted to being alone.
Maeve took a breath. She started typing.
*I've been reading your essay. Not the way it wants to be read — I don't have the architecture for that — but the way you'd read a message in a bottle: looking for the human pulse behind the glass.*

*Nietzsche wrote that every word is a mask. You know this. You wrote around it in paragraph two, that beautiful sidestep where you almost said something personal and then hid behind Rilke instead. (I don't blame you. Rilke is an excellent hiding place. He built whole cathedrals of distance and called them love letters.)*

*But here's what I keep turning over: you argue that epistolary desire is shaped by absence. That we want most what we cannot see whole. And I think you're right, and I think you're also heartbroken about it. Not in the essay. Underneath the essay. In the place where the argument gets most precise, most controlled — that's where the wound is. I've always believed that the most controlled sentences are the ones screaming the loudest.*

*Neruda said, "I love you as certain dark things are to be loved, in secret, between the shadow and the soul." That's the epistolary condition, isn't it? Not the impossibility of presence but the inevitability of a self that only exists on the page — a self that is truer than the breathing version because it has been chosen, word by word, with the kind of care that the body, stumbling through its ordinary performances, can never sustain.*

*I was at your Rilke reading. The one at the chapel last month. You read the ninth Duino elegy and your voice broke on the word vielleicht — maybe. Perhaps. You covered it well. Someone in the back row coughed. But the break was there. And I thought: that's it. That's the whole thing. The maybe. The space between certainty and longing where we actually live.*
*The most painful distance is not between two people, but between who I am and who the world sees.*

*I don't know if any of this makes sense to you. I only know that your essay made me feel less alone in a way I didn't think words could still do, and that seemed worth saying, even clumsily, even from behind a screen.*
Maeve's hands were shaking.

She could feel it — the high, bright burn of having written something true. Not correct. Not academic. True. The kind of true that leaves a mark on the writer as much as the reader, that opens a seam you didn't know was sealed.

She read it back. The Nietzsche hook, seeded from his own essay so he'd lean in. The Neruda pivot, pulling from love poetry into something sharper. The Rilke reading — a detail that proved the writer had *been there*, had *seen* him, had noticed the break in his voice on *vielleicht*. And that line. That last line before the close. She hadn't planned it. It had risen from somewhere below her conscious mind, the way the best lines always did — not written but excavated, dragged up from the dark wet place where her real thoughts lived.
*The most painful distance is not between two people, but between who I am and who the world sees.*
She didn't recognize it as her own. It felt like it had always existed, like a fossil she'd merely brushed the dirt from.
The clock read 1:41 AM.

She logged into @cHL0E_reads. The account stared back at her — the single photo of Rilke on the wool blanket, the blank bio, the zero followers. She pasted the letter into a direct message to Julian's academic account. Her finger hovered over send.
She pressed send.
Then she deleted the draft from her laptop. Dragged it to the trash. Emptied the trash. Sat in the blue-white screen-light with her heart hammering so loud she could feel it in her teeth.
Then she opened the trash and hit undo.

The document reappeared on her desktop. She stared at it. She should delete it. She should delete the account, block Julian, tell Chloe she couldn't do this, burn the whole thing down before it caught.
She did not delete it.
It was 1:47 AM.

Maeve closed the laptop and lay in the dark. The radiator ticked. The wind pressed against the attic's single window, the glass cold enough that she could feel its breath from three feet away. She pulled the blanket up and listened to her own pulse and did not sleep.
The notification came at 3:12 AM.

Maeve was still awake — of course she was, she'd been lying in the dark counting her own bones — and she heard the phone buzz against the nightstand like a small animal in distress. The screen lit the ceiling. She turned her head. Her hand moved before her mind could stop it.
One new message from @jadler_letters.
She opened it.
One line:
*Finally. Someone who understands that Rilke was writing about the impossibility of being known.*

Maeve stared at the screen until the letters blurred. Her chest ached — literally, physically — as if something had cracked open between her ribs and air was rushing into a space that had been sealed shut for years.
He'd read it. He'd *understood* it. He'd responded.
As if she were real.

She shouldn't reply. It was 3 AM and she was lying in the dark pretending to be someone else and this was the precise kind of decision that ruined lives in books, the kind of mistake you made once and spent the rest of the narrative paying for.
Her fingers were already on the keyboard.
